Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Left Fast, averaging 129.5 kph (up to 138.4).
  • Typical length is 7-8m (their good length); median 7.1 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length outside off, swinging both ways, seaming away, passing outside off (34% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 0% of the time against left-handers.
  • Across an over he holds a fairly consistent length, banging it in more as the over goes on.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Good Length (7 wkts, 1.2% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: 6th stump.
  • Takes 18% of wickets pitching a good length on the 5th stump.
  • Beats the bat 6.4% of tracked balls (false-shot 11.9%).
  • Gets you caught behind more than most — 91% of his wickets (1.3× the typical rate for this type).
  • 90% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, slip: 3rd, gully.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length wide outside off (21% of runs).
  • Short ball: 28% of deliveries, economy 4.5 — 2 wkts from 239 short balls.
  • Away from good length, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak through the leg side (38%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes full outside off (economy 4.8, beats the bat only 9%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs left-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length outside off, swinging both ways, seaming away, passing outside off (34% of deliveries, economy 1.24). Minor variations: a good length wide outside off (25%) and full outside off (13%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length outside off ▶swinging both ways, seaming away34%1.24413%
a good length wide outside off ▶swinging both ways, seaming both ways25%2.67213%
full outside off ▶swinging both ways, seaming away13%4.7639%
a good length in the channel ▶seaming away, swinging in6%2.75119%
back of a length outside off ▶seaming both ways4%5.19114%
back of a length wide outside off ▶seaming both ways, swinging4%7.9508%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Most threatening pitching a good length on the 6th stump (7 wkts, 1.2% strike). Most wickets come from a good length on the 5th stump, while he leaks the most runs a good length wide outside off.

Scoring Profile (vs left-handers)

52% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 15 balls; most runs go to the leg side (38%); the drive hurts most (21 fours/sixes at 2.7 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at stumps — pitches around 6th stump and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the off side (57%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length wide outside off most often (24%); mixes in the short ball (27%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length on the 5th stump, but strikes most often pitching on the 6th stump.

Speed & Spells

He is ~3.5 km/h quicker in later spells, is quicker in the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.
Speed by Spell
By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.
Speed by Day
By match day — fatigue across the game.

Sequencing & Over Construction

Moderately consistent with his length (length spread ±2.2 m; more repeatable than 54% of pace bowlers); tends to bang it in later in the over (short 4%→10% by the 5th–6th ball); and holds the same crease position through the over.
Across 19 dismissals, the wicket ball lands about 28 cm shorter than the ball before it — that's the change that brings the wicket.
Ball in overMedian lengthShort %EconWkt %

Ball 67.1 m10%3.612.1%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A high release point (taller than 76% of left-arm pace); releases about 54 cm from the middle stump; varies his spot on the crease a lot (more than 80% of left-arm pace). Comes wider round the wicket (54 cm over vs 89 cm round).

Movement (vs the average pace bowler · vs left-handers)

A hit-the-deck bowler who bangs it into a hard length for steep bounce.
Generates about average seam and about average swing for a pace bowler; seams it away more to left-hand batters (65%).
Swing by ball age to left-hand batters: new ball (≤25 ov, n=253) 49% away / 51% in; old ball (≥40 ov, n=174) 29% away / 71% in.

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
